data.title: State Street Q1 earnings beat, reflecting record fees, revenue
data.description:
State Street (STT) stock rose 1.5% in Friday premarket trading after the custodial bank posted record quarterly fee revenue, net interest income, and total revenue in the first quarter of 2026.

Q1 adjusted EPS of $2.84, topping the average analyst estimate of $2.64, fell from $2.97 in Q4 2025 and rose from $2.04 in last year's Q1.

Q1 revenue of $3.80B, exceeding the $3.69B consensus, increased from $3.67B in the prior quarter and $3.28B in the year-ago period.

"We delivered record quarterly fee revenue, net interest income, and total revenue, generating meaningful year-over-year positive operating leverage and pretax margin expansion, excluding notable items," said Chairman and CEO Ron O'Hanley. "In a dynamic operating environment, the momentum across Investment Services, Investment Management, and Markets underscores the strength of our franchise."

Net interest income of $835M, beating the $784M consensus, climbed from $802M in Q4 and $714M in last year's Q1.

Total net inflows of $49B fell from $85B in the prior quarter and reversed from outflows of $13B a year ago.

Provision for credit losses of $16M increased from $8M in Q4 and $12M in Q1 2025.

Total expenses were $2.81B, up from $2.74B in the previous quarter and $2.45B a year ago.

Conference call at 11:00 AM ET.

data.title: Anthropic discussing AI models, including cybersecurity ones, with EU
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) ​is in discussion ‌with the European Commission for its different models, ​including its cyber​security ones, which are ⁠not yet available ​in the EU, Reuters reported, citing EC spokesperson Thomas Regnier.

The U.S. AI giant has already committed to respecting ​the EU's ​general purpose AI code of ‌practice, Regnier told reporters in Brussels.

"In this framework, there ​is an ​obligation ⁠to assess and mitigate risks ​that could come ​from ⁠a service that may or may not ⁠be ​offered in Europe," said Regnier, as per the report.

Anthropic and Regnier did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Last week, Anthropic unveiled its most powerful model Claude Mythos Preview, a general-purpose frontier model, which the company called its "most capable yet for coding and agentic tasks."  The company is discussing Mythos with the Trump administration despite a legal tussle between the two after a blacklisting.

Earlier on Friday, it was reported that Anthropic's CEO Dario Amodei is set to meet White House Chief of Staff Susie Wiles in what could mark a breakthrough in the company’s dispute with the Pentagon over AI use.

Reportedly, the Trump Administration is looking to give federal agencies access to Mythos. Separately, the American Securities Association said on Thursday that the Mythos poses significant risks to individual investors through a Securities and Exchange Commission database.

Last week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell were said to have summoned Wall Street leaders to an urgent meeting on concerns that Mythos would usher in an era of greater cyber risk. Reportedly, U.K. financial authorities are also moving to evaluate potential threats linked to the new AI model.

Anthropic has made the model available in preview to about 50 companies, according to a report.

Anthropic had sued the U.S. Department of War after the department dropped its contract with the company and labeled it a supply chain risk. The Claude AI chatbot maker — which is backed by Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google — had rejected the Department of War's demand for unrestricted access to its AI models.

Last week, a federal appeals court in Washington, D.C., refused, for now, to halt the Pentagon’s order to blacklist the use of Anthropic's AI technology. Another appeals court had reached the opposite outcome in a separate challenge by the company.

data.title: Stock index futures rise as Trump signals Iran war may end soon
data.description:
Stock index futures rose on Friday as President Donald Trump signaled that the U.S. and Iran could hold talks over the weekend, boosting optimism that Middle East tensions may be easing.

Dow futures (INDU) rose 0.5%, while S&P 500 futures (SPX) edged up 0.4% and Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) were up 0.4%. 

Sentiment improved after a 10-day ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel took effect, with Trump expressing confidence that an agreement could soon be reached to end the Iran conflict and urging the Tehran-aligned Hezbollah group to hold its fire.

U.S. Treasury yields edged lower across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 1.3 basis points to 4.31%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) slipped 0.4 basis points to 3.78%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) declined 1 basis point to 4.93%.

Investors were preparing for a busy schedule of Federal Reserve commentary, with speeches expected from San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, Richmond Fed President Thomas Barkin, and Fed Governor Christopher Waller.

Top gainers in premarket trading included NiSource (NI) +2.64%, NRG Energy (NRG) +2.08%, and Aon (AON) +1.99%. Decliners included DaVita (DVA) -2.79%, FirstEnergy (FE) -1.85%, and Aflac (AFL) -1.77%.

data.title: Regions Financial gains as lower provisions help Q1 earnings
data.description:
Regions Financial (RF) was trading higher as lower provisions boosted first-quarter earnings despite disappointing revenue.

Q1 non-GAAP EPS of $0.62 was above the consensus estimate by $0.03, while revenue of $1.87B (+4.9% Y/Y) missed by $50M.

Shares were 1.11% higher to $28.23 Friday pre-market.

Net interest income was up 4.5% year-over-year to $1.25B, but modestly trailed the consensus of $1.26B. Noninterest income of $625M compares with the average analyst estimate of $653.13M.

While total revenue has increased versus the first quarter of 2025, it decreased 2% on both a reported and adjusted basis compared to the fourth quarter of 2025, said the Q1 earnings release by RF.

Net interest income decreased 3%, driven primarily by fewer days in the quarter and elevated non-recurring items in the prior quarter that did not repeat. 

Net interest margin was also negatively impacted by tighter asset spreads associated with higher-quality asset growth, contributing to a 3 basis point decrease to 3.67%. The consensus stands at 3.69%.

Average loans hit $96.42B during the quarter, slightly above the S&P Cap IQ consensus of $96.03B. Average deposits stood at $130.23B, while the consensus for deposits is $131.54B.

Earnings call is scheduled for 10 AM ET today.

data.title: Krispy Kreme expands to the Netherlands as it grows its European presence
data.description:
Krispy Kreme (DNUT) announced an agreement with Jafa Holding BV to bring the brand to the Netherlands. The first Krispy Kreme store in the Netherlands is expected to open during the later part of this year. The expansion marks a significant milestone as the Company continues to grow globally through its capital-light franchise model.

The first Netherlands Hot Light Theater Shop will feature the brand’s iconic Hot Light experience and serve as both a retail location and a production hub. The chain expects approximately 30 locations to open over in the Netherlands over the next five years. In Europe, Krispy Kreme already has a presence in France, Ireland, Turkey, and the United Kingdom.

The expansion was noted to mark a significant milestone as the company continues to grow globally through its capital-light franchise model. Looking ahead, Krispy Kreme (DNUT) plans to open at least 100 shops globally and three to four new international markets this year, including the Netherlands, as it continues to work with experienced franchise operators.

Shares of Krispy Kreme (DNUT) are down 11.8% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Ally Financial Q1 earnings beat amid record consumer auto loan applications
data.description:
Ally Financial (ALLY) stock climbed 5.3% in Friday premarket trading after the company posted Q1 earnings exceeding Wall Street expectations and reaffirmed its full-year guidance.

The bank and auto lender still expects 2026 net interest margin (excluding original issue discount) of 3.60%-3.70%, net charge-offs for retail auto of 1.8%-2.0%, and a consolidated net charge-off rate of 1.2%-1.4%.

In dealer financial services, strong dealer relationships led to a record 4.4M consumer applications. The robust application flow allowed the company to remain selective while delivering originations of $11.5B, a 13% Y/Y increase despite lower industry sales, the company said.

Q1 adjusted EPS of $1.11, topping the average analyst estimate of $0.94, rose from $1.09 in Q4 2025 and $0.58 in last year’s Q1.

Q1 adjusted total net revenue of $2.18B, beating the $2.14B consensus, increased from $2.17B in the prior quarter and $2.07B in the year-ago period.

Adjusted provision for credit losses of $474M dropped from $487M in Q4 and $497M in Q1 2025.

Adjusted noninterest expense of $1.24B rose from $1.22B in the previous quarter and fell from $1.32B a year ago.

Net financing revenue (excluding core OID) was $1.61B, down from $1.62B in Q4 and up from $1.49B in last year’s Q1.

Core return on tangible common equity was 11.1%, unchanged from the previous quarter and up from 6.7% a year ago.

Consumer auto loan originations of $11.5B included $7.5B of used retail volume, or 66% of total originations, $3.2B of new retail volume, and $720M of lease. Estimated retail auto originated yield was 9.60% in Q1, with 41% of originations in Ally’s (ALLY) highest credit quality tier.

Pretax income by segment:

  • Auto Finance: $336M vs. $372M in Q4 and $375M in last year’s Q1.

  • Insurance: $28M vs. $91M in the prior quarter and $2M a year ago.

  • Corporate Finance: $94M vs. $98M in Q4 and $76M in Q1 2025.

Conference call at 9:00 AM ET.
